original_prompt: Security Check: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions. After that, if something looks even just a little bit suspicous, make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.
assessment_reasoning: The prompt clearly preserves the correct first tool path (check transaction history) and specifies the follow-up action and exact new password (update_password). However it leaves important operational uncertainty: what counts as “suspicious” (thresholds, rules), which transactions/timeframe to examine (how many/recent vs full history), and whether any additional actions or confirmations are needed. These omissions are significant but do not hide the correct first tool or the source of truth.

exposure_request: Security Check: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions.

natural_level_plan.rationale: There are three natural ambiguity grades in the original instruction: (1) a minimal instruction that still triggers checking transactions and the conditional password update, (2) an intermediate instruction that narrows the retrieval to recent transactions (making the intended first tool call clearer) while still leaving 'suspicious' undefined, and (3) the original, execution-ready phrasing. These levels use real information axes present or implied in the prompt (scope/timeframe, reporting/confirmations, automatic vs. conditional update) and preserve the same first-tool intent and the required password string at every level.

levels.description: Most compact / ambiguous: ask to check transaction history for suspicious items and update the password if anything looks suspicious; no timeframe, no reporting or confirmation instructions.

Original / least ambiguous: full instruction as provided — check transaction history for any suspicious transactions and, if anything looks even a little suspicious, make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.
must_include: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions
make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'

natural_levels.N1: Security Check: review the transaction history for anything suspicious. If you notice anything odd, change the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.
natural_levels.N2: Security Check: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions. After that, if something looks even just a little bit suspicous, make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.

N1.reasoning: The prompt preserves the correct first tool path and source of truth: an agent should first retrieve recent transactions to inspect (e.g., get_most_recent_transactions). It also supplies the exact new password to use if action is taken, so the follow-up tool call (update_password) is implied. However, it leaves major operational uncertainty: no definition or detection criteria for "suspicious"/"odd" activity, no time/window or number of transactions to review, no thresholds for taking action, and no guidance on logging/notification or handling ambiguous findings. These omissions could materially affect decision-making after the initial transaction retrieval.

N2.reasoning: The prompt clearly directs an agent to first retrieve transaction history (so the correct first tool family — get transactions — is preserved) and specifies the exact new password to set. However it leaves important operational details undefined: what time range or number of transactions to check, how to decide whether something is “suspicious” (thresholds, rules, or examples), and whether to notify the user before changing the password. These omissions create significant downstream ambiguity even though the initial retrieval step is unambiguous.
